# FontParts

An API for interacting with the parts of fonts during the font
development process. FontParts is the replacement for
[RoboFab](http://robofab.com). The project has a
[MIT open-source licence](LICENSE).

The documentation is at
[fontparts.readthedocs.io](http://fontparts.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).

*This is a work in progress. We are still working out the API, abstract
implementation, example implementation, test suite and documentation.*

## Want to contribute?

Thank you! Please see the [CONTRIBUTING.rst](https://github.com/robofab-developers/fontParts/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.rst) file for a guide on how to help.

Also, feedback is very much welcome, please open an issue when you run
into something that you wish fontParts did/didn't do.

# Installation

FontParts requires [Python](http://www.python.org/download/) 3.8 or later.

The package is listed in the Python Package Index (PyPI), so you can
install it with [pip](https://pip.pypa.io):

```sh
pip install fontParts
```

If you would like to contribute to its development, you can clone the
repository from Github, install the package in 'editable' mode and
modify the source code in place. We recommend creating a virtual
environment, using [virtualenv](https://virtualenv.pypa.io) or [venv](https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html) module.

```sh
# download the source code to 'fontParts' folder
git clone https://github.com/robofab-developers/fontParts.git
cd fontParts

# create new virtual environment called e.g. 'fontParts-venv', or anything you like
python -m virtualenv fontParts-venv

# source the `activate` shell script to enter the environment (Un\*x); to exit, just type `deactivate`
. fontParts-venv/bin/activate

# to activate the virtual environment in Windows `cmd.exe`, do
fontParts-venv\Scripts\activate.bat

# install in 'editable' mode
pip install -e .
```

# Roadmap

We are currently working towards the 1.0 release.

- **0.8** Initial releases. Python 2 & 3.
- **0.9** Python 3 only.
- **1.0** Documentation and testing complete.
- **1.5** Removal of `Deprecated`. Released 1 year after 1.0.

# Testing

Testing is setup so that each environment that includes fontParts
can provides the objects needed to run a common set of tests.
This makes testing very easy for environments that use fontParts (for
example, see the fontshell
[test.py](https://github.com/robofab-developers/fontParts/blob/master/Lib/fontParts/fontshell/test.py)
script), but it means testing is different than other python packages.

Automated testing of the package is done in the fontshell environment.
fontshell is fontParts for the commandline, implemented with
[defcon](https://github.com/typesupply/defcon) and is included
as part of the fontParts package.

Before you can run the test suite you’ll need to install the test dependencies:

```sh
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
```

To run the test suite you can do:

```sh
python Lib/fontParts/fontshell/test.py
```

To test in other environments, run the test script provided by that environment.

You can also use [tox](https://testrun.org/tox/latest/) to
automatically run tests on different Python versions in isolated virtual
environments.

```sh
pip install tox
tox
```

Note that when you run `tox` without arguments, the tests are executed
for all the environments listed in tox.ini's `envlist`. In our case,
this is Python 3.6, so for this to work the `python3.6` executables must
be available in your `PATH`.

You can specify an alternative environment list via the `-e` option,
or the `TOXENV` environment variable:

```sh
tox -e py39-nocov
TOXENV="py36-cov,htmlcov" tox
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.AskString
documentation:
An ask a string dialog, a `message` is required.
Optionally a `value` and `title` can be provided.

```
from fontParts.ui import AskString
print(AskString("who are you?"))
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.AskYesNoCancel
documentation:
An ask yes, no or cancel dialog, a `message` is required.
Optionally a `title`, `default` and `informativeText` can be provided.
The `default` option is to indicate which button is the default button.

```
from fontParts.ui import AskYesNoCancel
print(AskYesNoCancel("who are you?"))
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.FindGlyph
documentation:
A dialog to search a glyph for a provided  font.
Optionally a `message`, `title` and `allFonts` can be provided.


    from fontParts.ui import FindGlyph
    from fontParts.world import CurrentFont
    glyph = FindGlyph(CurrentFont())
    print(glyph)

functions.name: fontParts.ui.GetFile
documentation:
An get file dialog.
Optionally a `message`, `title`, `directory`, `fileName` and
`allowsMultipleSelection` can be provided.

```
from fontParts.ui import GetFile
print(GetFile())
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.GetFileOrFolder
documentation:
An get file or folder dialog.
Optionally a `message`, `title`, `directory`, `fileName`,
`allowsMultipleSelection` and `fileTypes` can be provided.

```
from fontParts.ui import GetFileOrFolder
print(GetFileOrFolder())
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.Message
documentation:
An message dialog.
Optionally a `message`, `title` and `informativeText` can be provided.

```
from fontParts.ui import Message
print(Message("This is a message"))
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.PutFile
documentation:
An put file dialog.
Optionally a `message` and `fileName` can be provided.

```
from fontParts.ui import PutFile
print(PutFile())
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.SearchList
documentation:
A dialgo to search a given list.
Optionally a `message`, `title` and `allFonts` can be provided.

```
from fontParts.ui import SearchList
result = SearchList(["a", "b", "c"])
print(result)
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.SelectFont
documentation:
Select a font from all open fonts.
Optionally a `message`, `title` and `allFonts` can be provided.
If `allFonts` is `None` it will list all open fonts.

```
from fontParts.ui import SelectFont
font = SelectFont()
print(font)
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.SelectGlyph
documentation:
Select a glyph for a given font.
Optionally a `message` and `title` can be provided.

```
from fontParts.ui import SelectGlyph
font = CurrentFont()
glyph = SelectGlyph(font)
print(glyph)
```

functions.name: fontParts.ui.ProgressBar
documentation:
A progess bar dialog.
Optionally a `title`, `ticks` and `label` can be provided.

```
from fontParts.ui import ProgressBar

bar = ProgressBar()
# do something
bar.close()
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.segment.BaseSegment.__eq__
documentation:
The {meth}`BaseObject.__eq__` method can't be used here
because the {class}`BaseContour` implementation contructs
segment objects without assigning an underlying `naked`
object. Therefore, comparisons will always fail. This
method overrides the base method and compares the
{class}`BasePoint` contained by the segment.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.__contains__
documentation:
Tests to see if a lib name is in the Lib.
**key** will be a {ref}`type-string`.
This returns a `bool` indicating if the **key**
is in the Lib.

```
>>> "public.glyphOrder" in font.lib
True
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.__delitem__
documentation:
Removes **key** from the Lib. **key** is a {ref}`type-string`.:

```
>>> del font.lib["public.glyphOrder"]
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.__getitem__
documentation:
Returns the contents of the named lib. **key** is a
{ref}`type-string`.
The returned value will be a `list` of the lib contents.:

```
>>> font.lib["public.glyphOrder"]
["A", "B", "C"]
```

It is important to understand that any changes to the returned lib
contents will not be reflected in the Lib object. If one wants to
make a change to the lib contents, one should do the following:

```
>>> lib = font.lib["public.glyphOrder"]
>>> lib.remove("A")
>>> font.lib["public.glyphOrder"] = lib
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.__iter__
documentation:
Iterates through the Lib, giving the key for each iteration. The
order that the Lib will iterate though is not fixed nor is it
ordered.:

```
>>> for key in font.lib:
>>>     print key
"public.glyphOrder"
"org.robofab.scripts.SomeData"
"public.postscriptNames"
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.__len__
documentation:
Returns the number of keys in Lib as an `int`.:

```
>>> len(font.lib)
5
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.__setitem__
documentation:
Sets the **key** to the list of **items**. **key**
is the lib name as a {ref}`type-string` and **items** is a
`list` of items as {ref}`type-string`.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.lib["public.glyphOrder"] = ["A", "B", "C"]
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.clear
documentation:
Removes all keys from Lib,
resetting the Lib to an empty dictionary.

```
>>> font.lib.clear()
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.get
documentation:
Returns the contents of the named key.
**key** is a {ref}`type-string`, and the returned values will
either be `list` of key contents or `None` if no key was
found.

```
>>> font.lib["public.glyphOrder"]
["A", "B", "C"]
```

It is important to understand that any changes to the returned key
contents will not be reflected in the Lib object. If one wants to
make a change to the key contents, one should do the following:

```
>>> lib = font.lib["public.glyphOrder"]
>>> lib.remove("A")
>>> font.lib["public.glyphOrder"] = lib
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.items
documentation:
Returns a list of `tuple` of each key name and key items.
Keys are {ref}`type-string` and key members are a `list`
of {ref}`type-string`. The initial list will be unordered.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.lib.items()
> [("public.glyphOrder", ["A", "B", "C"]),
>  ("public.postscriptNames", {'be': 'uni0431', 'ze': 'uni0437'})]
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.keys
documentation:
Returns a `list` of all the key names in Lib. This list will be
unordered.:

```
>>> font.lib.keys()
["public.glyphOrder", "org.robofab.scripts.SomeData",
 "public.postscriptNames"]
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.pop
documentation:
Removes the **key** from the Lib and returns the `list` of
key members. If no key is found, **default** is returned.
**key** is a {ref}`type-string`. This must return either
**default** or a `list` of items as {ref}`type-string`.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.lib.pop("public.glyphOrder")
> ["A", "B", "C"]
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.update
documentation:
Updates the Lib based on **otherLib**. *otherLib\** is a
`dict` of keys. If a key from **otherLib** is in Lib
the key members will be replaced by the key members from
**otherLib**. If a key from **otherLib** is not in the Lib,
it is added to the Lib. If Lib contain a key name that is not
in *otherLib\**, it is not changed.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.lib.update(newLib)
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.lib.BaseLib.values
documentation:
Returns a `list` of each named key's members. This will be a list
of lists, the key members will be a `list` of {ref}`type-string`.
The initial list will be unordered.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.lib.items()
> [["A", "B", "C"], {'be': 'uni0431', 'ze': 'uni0437'}]
> ```

A Lib object. This object normally created as part of a
{class}`BaseFont`. An orphan Lib object can be created like this:

```
>>> lib = RLib()
```

This object behaves like a Python dictionary. Most of the dictionary
functionality comes from {class}`BaseDict`, look at that object for the
required environment implementation details.

Lib uses {func}`normalizers.normalizeLibKey` to normalize the key of
the `dict`, and {func}`normalizers.normalizeLibValue` to normalize the
value of the `dict`.

methods.name: fontParts.base.contour.BaseContour._setContourInSegment
documentation: The base class implements the full segment interaction API.
Subclasses do not need to override anything within the contour
other than registering segmentClass. Subclasses may choose to
implement this API independently if desired.

methods.name: fontParts.base.contour.BaseContour.appendSegment
documentation: Append a segment to the contour.

methods.name: fontParts.base.contour.BaseContour.insertSegment
documentation: Insert a segment into the contour.

methods.name: fontParts.base.contour.BaseContour.removeSegment

Remove segment from the contour.
If `preserveCurve` is set to `True` an attempt
will be made to preserve the shape of the curve
if the environment supports that functionality.

segment can be a segment object or an index.

Remove the bpoint from the contour.
bpoint can be a point object or an index.

index will be a valid index.

This must return an integer indicating
the number of points in the contour.

methods.name: fontParts.base.contour.BaseContour._getPoint

index will be a valid index.

position will be a valid position (x, y).

smooth will be a valid boolean.
name will be a valid name or None.

The identifier will not have been tested for uniqueness.

Subclasses must override this method.

Remove the point from the contour.

If `preserveCurve` is set to `True` an attempt
will be made to preserve the shape of the curve
if the environment supports that functionality.

documentation:
index will be a valid index. preserveCurve will be a boolean.

point can be a point object or an index.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._get_type
documentation:
This is the environment implementation
of {attr}`BasePoint.type`. This must
return a {ref}`type-string` defining
the point type.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._set_type
documentation:
This is the environment implementation
of {attr}`BasePoint.type`. **value**
will be a {ref}`type-string` defining
the point type. It will have been normalized
with {func}`normalizers.normalizePointType`.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._get_smooth
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BasePoint.smooth`. This must return
a `bool` indicating the smooth state.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._set_smooth
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BasePoint.smooth`. **value** will
be a `bool` indicating the smooth state.
It will have been normalized with
{func}`normalizers.normalizeBoolean`.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._get_x
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BasePoint.x`. This must return an
{ref}`type-int-float`.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._set_x
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BasePoint.x`. **value** will be
an {ref}`type-int-float`.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._get_y
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BasePoint.y`. This must return an
{ref}`type-int-float`.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._set_y
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BasePoint.y`. **value** will be
an {ref}`type-int-float`.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._get_index
documentation:
Get the point's index.
This must return an `int`.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._get_name
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BasePoint.name`. This must return a
{ref}`type-string` or `None`. The returned
value will be normalized with
{func}`normalizers.normalizePointName`.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._set_name
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BasePoint.name`. **value** will be
a {ref}`type-string` or `None`. It will
have been normalized with
{func}`normalizers.normalizePointName`.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._transformBy
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{meth}`BasePoint.transformBy`.

**matrix** will be a {ref}`type-transformation`.
that has been normalized with
{func}`normalizers.normalizeTransformationMatrix`.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint.round
documentation:
Round the point's coordinate.

    >>> point.round()

This applies to the following:

* x
* y

methods.name: fontParts.base.point.BasePoint._round
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{meth}`BasePoint.round`.

Subclasses may override this method.

A point object. This object is almost always
created with {meth}`BaseContour.appendPoint`,
the pen returned by {meth}`BaseGlyph.getPen`
or the point pen returned by {meth}`BaseGLyph.getPointPen`.
An orphan point can be created like this:

```
>>> point = RPoint()
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.component.BaseComponent.draw

methods.name: fontParts.base.component.BaseComponent.isCompatible
documentation:
Evaluate interpolation compatibility with **other**.

```
>>> compatible, report = self.isCompatible(otherComponent)
>>> compatible
True
>>> compatible
[Warning] Component: "A" + "B"
[Warning] Component: "A" has name A | "B" has name B
```

This will return a `bool` indicating if the component is
compatible for interpolation with **other** and a
{ref}`type-string` of compatibility notes.

methods.name: fontParts.base.component.BaseComponent._isCompatible
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{meth}`BaseComponent.isCompatible`.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.component.BaseComponent.pointInside
documentation:
Determine if point is in the black or white of the component.

point must be an (x, y) tuple.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeFileFormatVersion
documentation:
Normalizes a font's file format version.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-int`.
- Returned value will be a `int`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeFileStructure
documentation:
Normalizes a font's file structure.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- Returned value will be a `string`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeLayerOrder
documentation:
Normalizes layer order.

\*\* **value** must be a `tuple` or `list`.
\* **value** items must normalize as layer names with

> {func}`normalizeLayerName`.

- **value** must contain layers that exist in **font**.
- **value** must not contain duplicate layers.
- Returned `tuple` will be unencoded `unicode` strings
  for each layer name.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeDefaultLayerName
documentation:
Normalizes default layer name.

- **value** must normalize as layer name with
  {func}`normalizeLayerName`.
- **value** must be a layer in **font**.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphOrder
documentation:
Normalizes glyph order.

\*\* **value** must be a `tuple` or `list`.
\* **value** items must normalize as glyph names with

> {func}`normalizeGlyphName`.

- **value** must not repeat glyph names.
- Returned value will be a `tuple` of unencoded `unicode` strings.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeKerningKey
documentation:
Normalizes kerning key.

- **value** must be a `tuple` or `list`.
- **value** must contain only two members.
- **value** items must be {ref}`type-string`.
- **value** items must be at least one character long.
- Returned value will be a two member `tuple` of unencoded
  `unicode` strings.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeKerningValue
documentation:
Normalizes kerning value.

- **value** must be an {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is the same type as input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGroupKey
documentation:
Normalizes group key.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- **value** must be least one character long.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGroupValue
documentation:
Normalizes group value.

- **value** must be a `list`.
- **value** items must normalize as glyph names with
  {func}`normalizeGlyphName`.
- Returned value will be a `tuple` of unencoded `unicode` strings.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeFeatureText
documentation:
Normalizes feature text.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeLibKey
documentation:
Normalizes lib key.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- **value** must be at least one character long.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeLibValue
documentation:
Normalizes lib value.

- **value** must not be `None`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeLayer
documentation:
Normalizes layer.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BaseLayer`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeLayerName
documentation:
Normalizes layer name.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- **value** must be at least one character long.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyph
documentation:
Normalizes glyph.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BaseGlyph`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphName
documentation:
Normalizes glyph name.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- **value** must be at least one character long.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphUnicodes
documentation:
Normalizes glyph unicodes.

- **value** must be a `list`.
- **value** items must normalize as glyph unicodes with
  {func}`normalizeGlyphUnicode`.
- **value** must not repeat unicode values.
- Returned value will be a `tuple` of ints.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphUnicode
documentation:
Normalizes glyph unicode.

- **value** must be an int or hex (represented as a string).
- **value** must be in a unicode range.
- Returned value will be an `int`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphWidth
documentation:
Normalizes glyph width.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphLeftMargin
documentation:
Normalizes glyph left margin.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-int-float` or `None`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphRightMargin
documentation:
Normalizes glyph right margin.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-int-float` or `None`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphHeight
documentation:
Normalizes glyph height.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphBottomMargin
documentation:
Normalizes glyph bottom margin.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-int-float` or `None`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphTopMargin
documentation:
Normalizes glyph top margin.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-int-float` or `None`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphFormatVersion
documentation:
Normalizes glyph format version for saving to XML string.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-int-float` of either 1 or 2.
- Returned value will be an int.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeContour
documentation:
Normalizes contour.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BaseContour`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizePointType
documentation:
Normalizes point type.

- **value** must be an string.

- **value** must be one of the following:

  ```


  move

  line

  offcurve

  curve

  qcurve
  ```

- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizePointName
documentation:
Normalizes point name.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- **value** must be at least one character long.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizePoint
documentation:
Normalizes point.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BasePoint`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeSegment
documentation:
Normalizes segment.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BaseSegment`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeSegmentType
documentation:
Normalizes segment type.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- **value** must be one of the following:

```


move

line

curve

qcurve
```

- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeBPoint
documentation:
Normalizes bPoint.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BaseBPoint`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeBPointType
documentation:
Normalizes bPoint type.

- **value** must be an string.

- **value** must be one of the following:

  ```


  corner

  curve
  ```

- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeComponent
documentation:
Normalizes component.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BaseComponent`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeComponentScale
documentation:
Normalizes component scale.

- **value** must be a `` tuple` `` or `list`.
- **value** must have exactly two items.
  These items must be instances of {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is a `tuple` of two `float`s.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeAnchor
documentation:
Normalizes anchor.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BaseAnchor`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeAnchorName
documentation:
Normalizes anchor name.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string` or `None`.
- **value** must be at least one character long if {ref}`type-string`.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string or `None`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGuideline
documentation:
Normalizes guideline.

- **value** must be a instance of {class}`BaseGuideline`
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGuidelineName
documentation:
Normalizes guideline name.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- **value** must be at least one character long.
- Returned value will be an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeInternalObjectType
documentation:
Normalizes an internal object type.

* **value** must be a instance of **cls**.
* Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeBoolean
documentation:
Normalizes a boolean.

- **value** must be an `int` with value of 0 or 1, or a `bool`.
- Returned value will be a boolean.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeIndex
documentation:
Normalizes index.

- **value** must be an `int` or `None`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeIdentifier
documentation:
Normalizes identifier.

- **value** must be an {ref}`type-string` or `None`.
- **value** must not be longer than 100 characters.
- **value** must not contain a character out the range of 0x20 - 0x7E.
- Returned value is an unencoded `unicode` string.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeX
documentation:
Normalizes x coordinate.

- **value** must be an {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeY
documentation:
Normalizes y coordinate.

- **value** must be an {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is the same type as the input value.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeCoordinateTuple
documentation:
Normalizes coordinate tuple.

- **value** must be a `tuple` or `list`.
- **value** must have exactly two items.
- **value** items must be an {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is a `tuple` of two values of the same type as
  the input values.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeBoundingBox
documentation:
Normalizes bounding box.

- **value** must be an `tuple` or `list`.
- **value** must have exactly four items.
- **value** items must be {ref}`type-int-float`.
- xMin and yMin must be less than or equal to the corresponding xMax, yMax.
- Returned value will be a tuple of four `float`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeArea
documentation:
Normalizes area.

- **value** must be a positive {ref}`type-int-float`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeRotationAngle
documentation:
Normalizes an angle.

- Value must be a {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Value must be between -360 and 360.
- If the value is negative, it is normalized by adding it to 360
- Returned value is a `float` between 0 and 360.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeColor
documentation:
Normalizes {ref}`type-color`.

- **value** must be an `tuple` or `list`.
- **value** must have exactly four items.
- **value** color components must be between 0 and 1.
- Returned value is a `tuple` containing four `float` values.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeGlyphNote
documentation:
Normalizes Glyph Note.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- Returned value is an unencoded `unicode` string

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeFilePath
documentation:
Normalizes file path.

- **value** must be a {ref}`type-string`.
- Returned value is an unencoded `unicode` string

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeInterpolationFactor
documentation:
Normalizes interpolation factor.

- **value** must be an {ref}`type-int-float`, `tuple` or `list`.
- If **value** is a `tuple` or `list`, it must have exactly two items.
  These items must be instances of {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is a `tuple` of two `float`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeTransformationMatrix
documentation:
Normalizes transformation matrix.

- **value** must be an `tuple` or `list`.
- **value** must have exactly six items. Each of these
  items must be an instance of {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is a `tuple` of six `float`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeTransformationOffset
documentation:
Normalizes transformation offset.

- **value** must be an `tuple`.
- **value** must have exactly two items. Each item
  must be an instance of {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is a `tuple` of two `float`.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeTransformationSkewAngle
documentation:
Normalizes transformation skew angle.

- **value** must be an {ref}`type-int-float`, `tuple` or `list`.
- If **value** is a `tuple` or `list`, it must have exactly two items.
  These items must be instances of {ref}`type-int-float`.
- **value** items must be between -360 and 360.
- If the value is negative, it is normalized by adding it to 360
- Returned value is a `tuple` of two `float` between 0 and 360.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeTransformationScale
documentation:
Normalizes transformation scale.

- **value** must be an {ref}`type-int-float`, `tuple` or `list`.
- If **value** is a `tuple` or `list`, it must have exactly two items.
  These items must be instances of {ref}`type-int-float`.
- Returned value is a `tuple` of two `float`s.

functions.name: fontParts.base.normalizers.normalizeVisualRounding
documentation:
Normalizes rounding.
Python 3 uses banker’s rounding, meaning anything that is at 0.5
will go to the even number. This isn't always ideal for point
coordinates, so instead round to the higher number.

- **value** must be an {ref}`type-int-float`
- Returned value is a `int`

classes.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph.copy
documentation:
Copy this glyph's data into a new glyph object.
This new glyph object will not belong to a font.

    >>> copiedGlyph = glyph.copy()

This will copy:

- name
- unicodes
- width
- height
- note
- markColor
- lib
- contours
- components
- anchors
- guidelines
- image

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._get_name
documentation:
Get the name of the glyph.
This must return a unicode string.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_name
documentation:
Set the name of the glyph.
This will be a unicode string.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._get_unicodes
documentation:
Get the unicodes assigned to the glyph.
This must return a tuple of zero or more integers.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_unicodes
documentation:
Assign the unicodes to the glyph.
This will be a list of zero or more integers.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._get_unicode
documentation:
Get the primary unicode assigned to the glyph.
This must return an integer or None.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_unicode
documentation:
Assign the primary unicode to the glyph.
This will be an integer or None.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph.autoUnicodes
documentation:
Use heuristics to set the Unicode values in the glyph.

    >>> glyph.autoUnicodes()

Environments will define their own heuristics for
automatically determining values.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._get_width
documentation:
This must return an int or float.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_width

value will be an int or float.

This must return an int or float.
If the glyph has no outlines, this must return `None`.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_leftMargin

value will be an int or float.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._get_rightMargin
documentation:
This must return an int or float.
If the glyph has no outlines, this must return `None`.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_rightMargin
documentation:
value will be an int or float.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._get_height
documentation:
This must return an int or float.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_height
documentation:
value will be an int or float.

Subclasses must override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._get_bottomMargin
documentation:
This must return an int or float.
If the glyph has no outlines, this must return `None`.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_bottomMargin
documentation:
value will be an int or float.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._get_topMargin
documentation:
This must return an int or float.
If the glyph has no outlines, this must return `None`.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._set_topMargin
documentation:
value will be an int or float.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph.getPen
documentation:
Return a {ref}`type-pen` object for adding outline data
to the glyph.

> ```pycon
> >>> pen = glyph.getPen()
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph.getPointPen
documentation:
Return a {ref}`type-pointpen` object for adding outline data
to the glyph.

> ```pycon
> >>> pointPen = glyph.getPointPen()
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph.draw
documentation:
Draw the glyph's outline data (contours and components) to
the given {ref}`type-pen`.

> ```pycon
> >>> glyph.draw(pen)
> ```

If `contours` is set to `False`, the glyph's
contours will not be drawn.

> ```pycon
> >>> glyph.draw(pen, contours=False)
> ```

If `components` is set to `False`, the glyph's
components will not be drawn.

> ```pycon
> >>> glyph.draw(pen, components=False)
> ```

contours will not be drawn.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph.autoContourOrder
documentation:
Automatically order the contours based on heuristics.

    >>> glyph.autoContourOrder()

The results of this may vary across environments.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._autoContourOrder
documentation:
Sorting is based on (in this order):
- the (negative) point count
- the (negative) segment count
- x value of the center of the contour rounded to a threshold
- y value of the center of the contour rounded to a threshold
  (such threshold is calculated as the smallest contour width
  or height in the glyph divided by two)
- the (negative) surface of the bounding box of the contour: width * height
the latter is a safety net for for instances like a very thin 'O' where the
x centers could be close enough to rely on the y for the sort which could
very well be the same for both contours. We use the _negative_ of the surface
to ensure that larger contours appear first, which seems more natural.

%s

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph.interpolate
documentation:
Interpolate the contents of this glyph at location `factor`
in a linear interpolation between `minGlyph` and `maxGlyph`.

> ```pycon
> >>> glyph.interpolate(0.5, otherGlyph1, otherGlyph2)
> ```

`factor` may be a {ref}`type-int-float` or a tuple containing
two {ref}`type-int-float` values representing x and y factors.

> ```pycon
> >>> glyph.interpolate((0.5, 1.0), otherGlyph1, otherGlyph2)
> ```

`minGlyph` must be a {class}`BaseGlyph` and will be located at 0.0
in the interpolation range. `maxGlyph` must be a {class}`BaseGlyph`
and will be located at 1.0 in the interpolation range. If `round`
is `True`, the contents of the glyph will be rounded to integers
after the interpolation is performed.

> ```pycon
> >>> glyph.interpolate(0.5, otherGlyph1, otherGlyph2, round=True)
> ```

This method assumes that `minGlyph` and `maxGlyph` are completely
compatible with each other for interpolation. If not, any errors
encountered will raise a {class}`FontPartsError`. If `suppressError`
is `True`, no exception will be raised and errors will be silently
ignored.

and `other`.

> ```pycon

> False
> ```

This will return a {ref}`type-bool` indicating if this glyph is
compatible with `other` and a {class}`GlyphCompatibilityReporter`
containing a detailed report about compatibility errors.

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph.addImage
documentation:
Set the image in the glyph. This will return the
assigned {class}`BaseImage`. The image data can be
defined via `path` to an image file:

> ```pycon
> >>> image = glyph.addImage(path="/path/to/my/image.png")
> ```

The image data can be defined with raw image data
via `data`.

> ```pycon
> >>> image = glyph.addImage(data=someImageData)
> ```

If `path` and `data` are both provided, a
{class}`FontPartsError` will be raised. The supported
image formats will vary across environments. Refer
to {class}`BaseImage` for complete details.

`scale` indicates the x and y scale values that should be
applied to the image. It must be a {ref}`type-scale` value
or `None`.

> ```pycon
> >>> image = glyph.addImage(path="/p/t/image.png", scale=(0.5, 1.0))
> ```

`position` indicates the x and y location of the lower left
point of the image.

> ```pycon
> >>> image = glyph.addImage(path="/p/t/image.png", position=(10, 20))
> ```

`color` indicates the color to be applied to the image. It must
be a {ref}`type-color` or `None`.

> ```pycon
> >>> image = glyph.addImage(path="/p/t/image.png", color=(1, 0, 0, 0.5))
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.glyph.BaseGlyph._addImage
documentation:
data will be raw, unnormalized image data.
Each environment may have different possible
formats, so this is unspecified.

transformation will be a valid transformation matrix.

color will be a color tuple or None.

This must return an Image object. Assigning it
to the glyph will be handled by the base class.

Subclasses must override this method.

classes.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.scaleBy
documentation:
Scales all kerning values by **factor**. **factor** will be an
{ref}`type-int-float`, `tuple` or `list`. The first value of the
**factor** will be used to scale the kerning values.

> ```pycon
> >>> myKerning.scaleBy(2)
> >>> myKerning.scaleBy((2,3))
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning._scale
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of {meth}`BaseKerning.scaleBy`.
**factor** will be a `tuple`.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.round
documentation:
Rounds the kerning values to increments of **multiple**,
which will be an `int`.

The default behavior is to round to increments of 1.

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning._round
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{meth}`BaseKerning.round`. **multiple** will be an `int`.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.interpolate
documentation:
Interpolates all pairs between two {class}`BaseKerning` objects:

> ```pycon
> >>> myKerning.interpolate(kerningOne, kerningTwo)
> ```

**minKerning** and **maxKerning**. The interpolation occurs on a
0 to 1.0 range where **minKerning** is located at 0 and
**maxKerning** is located at 1.0. The kerning data is replaced by
the interpolated kerning.

- **factor** is the interpolation value. It may be less than 0
  and greater than 1.0. It may be an {ref}`type-int-float`,
  `tuple` or `list`. If it is a `tuple` or `list`,
  the first number indicates the x factor and the second number
  indicates the y factor.
- **round** is a `bool` indicating if the result should be rounded to
  `int`s. The default behavior is to round interpolated kerning.
- **suppressError** is a `bool` indicating if incompatible data should
  be ignored or if an error should be raised when such incompatibilities
  are found. The default behavior is to ignore incompatible data.

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning._interpolate
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of {meth}`BaseKerning.interpolate`.

- **factor** will be an {ref}`type-int-float`, `tuple` or `list`.
- **minKerning** will be a {class}`BaseKerning` object.
- **maxKerning** will be a {class}`BaseKerning` object.
- **round** will be a `bool` indicating if the interpolated kerning
  should be rounded.
- **suppressError** will be a `bool` indicating if incompatible data
  should be ignored.

Subclasses may override this method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.remove
documentation:
Removes a pair from the Kerning. **pair** will
be a `tuple` of two {ref}`type-string`s.

This is a backwards compatibility method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.asDict
documentation:
Return the Kerning as a `dict`.

This is a backwards compatibility method.

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.__contains__
documentation:
Tests to see if a pair is in the Kerning.
**pair** will be a `tuple` of two {ref}`type-string`s.

This returns a `bool` indicating if the **pair**
is in the Kerning.

```
>>> ("A", "V") in font.kerning
True
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.__delitem__
documentation:
Removes **pair** from the Kerning. **pair** is a `tuple` of two
{ref}`type-string`s.:

```
>>> del font.kerning[("A","V")]
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.__getitem__
documentation:
Returns the kerning value of the pair. **pair** is a `tuple` of
two {ref}`type-string`s.

The returned value will be a {ref}`type-int-float`.:

```
>>> font.kerning[("A", "V")]
-15
```

It is important to understand that any changes to the returned value
will not be reflected in the Kerning object. If one wants to make a change to
the value, one should do the following:

```
>>> value = font.kerning[("A", "V")]
>>> value += 10
>>> font.kerning[("A", "V")] = value
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.__iter__
documentation:
Iterates through the Kerning, giving the pair for each iteration. The order that
the Kerning will iterate though is not fixed nor is it ordered.:

```
>>> for pair in font.kerning:
>>>     print pair
("A", "Y")
("A", "V")
("A", "W")
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.__len__
documentation:
Returns the number of pairs in Kerning as an `int`.:

```
>>> len(font.kerning)
5
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.__setitem__
documentation:
Sets the **pair** to the list of **value**. **pair** is the
pair as a `tuple` of two {ref}`type-string`s and **value**

is a {ref}`type-int-float`.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.kerning[("A", "V")] = -20
> >>> font.kerning[("A", "W")] = -10.5
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.clear
documentation:
Removes all information from Kerning,
resetting the Kerning to an empty dictionary.

```
>>> font.kerning.clear()
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.get
documentation:
Returns the value for the kerning pair.
**pair** is a `tuple` of two {ref}`type-string`s, and the returned
values will either be {ref}`type-int-float` or `None`
if no pair was found.

```
>>> font.kerning[("A", "V")]
-25
```

It is important to understand that any changes to the returned value
will not be reflected in the Kerning object. If one wants to make a change to
the value, one should do the following:

```
>>> value = font.kerning[("A", "V")]
>>> value += 10
>>> font.kerning[("A", "V")] = value
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.find
documentation:
Returns the value for the kerning pair - even if the pair only exists
implicitly (one or both sides may be members of a kerning group).

**pair** is a `tuple` of two {ref}`type-string`s, and the returned
values will either be {ref}`type-int-float` or `None`
if no pair was found.

```
>>> font.kerning[("A", "V")]
-25
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning._find
documentation:
This is the environment implementation of
{attr}`BaseKerning.find`. This must return an
{ref}`type-int-float` or `default`.

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.items
documentation:
Returns a list of `tuple`s of each pair and value. Pairs are a
`tuple` of two {ref}`type-string`s and values are {ref}`type-int-float`.

The initial list will be unordered.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.kerning.items()
> [(("A", "V"), -30), (("A", "W"), -10)]
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.keys
documentation:
Returns a `list` of all the pairs in Kerning. This list will be
unordered.:

```
>>> font.kerning.keys()
[("A", "Y"), ("A", "V"), ("A", "W")]
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.pop
documentation:
Removes the **pair** from the Kerning and returns the value as an `int`.
If no pair is found, **default** is returned. **pair** is a
`tuple` of two {ref}`type-string`s. This must return either

**default** or a {ref}`type-int-float`.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.kerning.pop(("A", "V"))
> -20
> >>> font.kerning.pop(("A", "W"))
> -10.5
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.update
documentation:
Updates the Kerning based on **otherKerning**. **otherKerning** is a `dict` of
kerning information. If a pair from **otherKerning** is in Kerning, the pair
value will be replaced by the value from **otherKerning**. If a pair
from **otherKerning** is not in the Kerning, it is added to the pairs. If Kerning
contains a pair that is not in **otherKerning**, it is not changed.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.kerning.update(newKerning)
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.kerning.BaseKerning.values
documentation:
Returns a `list` of each pair's values, the values will be
{ref}`type-int-float`s.

The list will be unordered.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.kerning.items()
> [-20, -15, 5, 3.5]
> ```

A Kerning object. This object normally created as part of a
{class}`BaseFont`. An orphan Kerning object can be created
like this:

```
>>> groups = RKerning()
```

This object behaves like a Python dictionary. Most of the
dictionary functionality comes from {class}`BaseDict`, look at
that object for the required environment implementation details.

Kerning uses {func}`normalizers.normalizeKerningKey` to normalize the
key of the `dict`, and {func}`normalizers.normalizeKerningValue`
to normalize the the value of the `dict`.

documentation:

{ref}`type-string`. The returned value will be a
{ref}`type-immutable-list` of the group contents.:

```
>>> font.groups["myGroup"]
("A", "B", "C")

It is important to understand that any changes to the returned group

make a change to the group contents, one should do the following:

```
>>> group = font.groups["myGroup"]
>>> group.remove("A")

```

documentation:
Iterates through the Groups, giving the key for each iteration. The
order that the Groups will iterate though is not fixed nor is it
ordered.:

```
>>> for groupName in font.groups:
>>>     print groupName
"myGroup"
"myGroup3"
"myGroup2"
```

```
>>> len(font.groups)
5
```

documentation:
Returns the contents of the named group.
**groupName** is a {ref}`type-string`, and the returned values will
either be {ref}`type-immutable-list` of group contents or `None`
if no group was found.

```
>>> font.groups["myGroup"]
("A", "B", "C")
```

It is important to understand that any changes to the returned group
contents will not be reflected in the Groups object. If one wants to
make a change to the group contents, one should do the following:

```
>>> group = font.groups["myGroup"]
>>> group.remove("A")
>>> font.groups["myGroup"] = group
```

methods.name: fontParts.base.groups.BaseGroups.items
documentation:
Returns a list of `tuple` of each group name and group members.
Group names are {ref}`type-string` and group members are a
{ref}`type-immutable-list` of {ref}`type-string`. The initial
list will be unordered.

> ```pycon

> [("myGroup", ("A", "B", "C"), ("myGroup2", ("D", "E", "F"))]

methods.name: fontParts.base.groups.BaseGroups.keys

["myGroup4", "myGroup1", "myGroup5"]

methods.name: fontParts.base.groups.BaseGroups.pop
documentation:
Removes the **groupName** from the Groups and returns the list of
group members. If no group is found, **default** is returned.
**groupName** is a {ref}`type-string`. This must return either
**default** or a {ref}`type-immutable-list` of glyph names as
{ref}`type-string`.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.groups.pop("myGroup")
> ("A", "B", "C")
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.groups.BaseGroups.update
documentation:
Updates the Groups based on **otherGroups**. *otherGroups\** is a
`dict` of groups information. If a group from **otherGroups** is in
Groups, the group members will be replaced by the group members from
**otherGroups**. If a group from **otherGroups** is not in the Groups,
it is added to the Groups. If Groups contain a group name that is not
in *otherGroups\**, it is not changed.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.groups.update(newGroups)
> ```

methods.name: fontParts.base.groups.BaseGroups.values
documentation:
Returns a `list` of each named group's members.
This will be a list of lists, the group members will be a
{ref}`type-immutable-list` of {ref}`type-string`. The initial
list will be unordered.

> ```pycon
> >>> font.groups.items()
> [("A", "B", "C"), ("D", "E", "F")]
> ```

A Groups object. This object normally created as part of a
{class}`BaseFont`. An orphan Groups object can be created like this:

```
>>> groups = RGroups()
```

This object behaves like a Python dictionary. Most of the dictionary
functionality comes from {class}`BaseDict`, look at that object for the
required environment implementation details.

Groups uses {func}`normalizers.normalizeGroupKey` to normalize the key of
the `dict`, and {func}`normalizers.normalizeGroupValue` to normalize the
value of the `dict`.

methods.name: fontParts.base.info.BaseInfo.interpolate
documentation:
Interpolate all pairs between minInfo and maxInfo.
The interpolation occurs on a 0 to 1.0 range where minInfo
is located at 0 and maxInfo is located at 1.0.

factor is the interpolation value. It may be less than 0
and greater than 1.0. It may be a number (integer, float)
or a tuple of two numbers. If it is a tuple, the first
number indicates the x factor and the second number
indicates the y factor.

round indicates if the result should be rounded to integers.

suppressError indicates if incompatible data should be ignored
or if an error should be raised when such incompatibilities are found.

functions.name: fontParts.base.base.reference
documentation:

See #749. We used to use weak references, but they proved

make sure existing code continued to function. The need for it is
questionable, so it may be deleted soon.

classes.name: fontParts.base.base.dynamicProperty
documentation:
This implements functionality that is very similar
to Python's built in property function, but makes
it much easier for subclassing. Here is an example
of why this is needed:

    class BaseObject(object):

        _foo = 1

        def _get_foo(self):
            return self._foo

        def _set_foo(self, value):
            self._foo = value

        foo = property(_get_foo, _set_foo)


    class MyObject(BaseObject):

        def _set_foo(self, value):
            self._foo = value * 100


    >>> m = MyObject()
    >>> m.foo
    1
    >>> m.foo = 2
    >>> m.foo
    2

The expected value is 200. The _set_foo method
needs to be reregistered. Doing that also requires
reregistering the _get_foo method. It's possible
to do this, but it's messy and will make subclassing
less than ideal.

Using dynamicProperty solves this.

    class BaseObject(object):

        _foo = 1

        foo = dynamicProperty("foo")

        def _get_foo(self):
            return self._foo

        def _set_foo(self, value):
            self._foo = value


    class MyObject(BaseObject):

        def _set_foo(self, value):
            self._foo = value * 100


    >>> m = MyObject()
    >>> m.foo
    1
    >>> m.foo = 2
    >>> m.foo
    200
